syllabus.topic: Introduction to Clustering in Pattern Recognition
sub_points: Positioning clustering within pattern representation learning and unsupervised pattern recognition
Core definition of clustering and typical objectives (discovering structure, grouping similar samples)
Common application motivations and how clustering differs from classification (no labels, exploratory)
Hard clustering concept: exclusive assignment of each data point to exactly one cluster
Major families: partitional vs hierarchical clustering (and how outputs differ: partitions vs dendrograms)
Cluster notions/criteria: well-separated, center-based, contiguous (nearest-neighbor), and density-based clusters
Choosing the number of clusters (illustrated via two-cluster examples and separability intuition)

syllabus.topic: Partitional Clustering with K-Means (Algorithm and Workflow)
sub_points: K-means objective as within-cluster compactness (distance to centroids) and iterative optimization
Algorithm steps: initialization, assignment step, centroid update step, and convergence/stopping conditions
Practical considerations: distance metrics (typically Euclidean), sensitivity to initialization, and local minima
Worked iterations/examples demonstrating how assignments and centroids change until no further changes occur

syllabus.topic: Limitations and Failure Modes of K-Means
sub_points: Sensitivity to initialization and convergence to local optima; variability across runs
Assumption of roughly spherical, equal-variance clusters; difficulty with non-convex or elongated structures
Vulnerability to outliers and scale issues (features must often be normalized)
Requirement to pre-specify K and challenges when clusters overlap or have different densities/sizes

syllabus.topic: Hierarchical Clustering: Agglomerative Methods and Linkage Criteria
sub_points: Hierarchical clustering overview (agglomerative bottom-up approach) and dendrogram interpretation
Single-link (MIN) linkage: cluster similarity defined by the closest pair across clusters; chaining effect
Complete-link (MAX) linkage: similarity defined by the farthest pair; produces tighter clusters
Group-average linkage: average pairwise proximity between clusters; trade-off between MIN and MAX behaviors
Centroid linkage: distance between cluster centroids; how centroid updates affect merge decisions

syllabus.topic: Evaluating and Comparing Hierarchical Clustering; Limitations
sub_points: Limitations of agglomerative clustering (greedy/irreversible merges, computational cost, noise sensitivity)
Use of numerical measures to judge/compare clustering outcomes or linkage behaviors
Worked example with a small dataset (e.g., 6 points) comparing MIN, MAX, and Group Average merge behavior
Interpretation of distances/proximities and how linkage choice changes the resulting hierarchy
